content.problemStatement:
Utkarsh is currently sitting for placements. He has applied to three companies named A,BA, BA,B, and CCC.

You know Utkarsh's order of preference among these 333 companies, given to you as characters `first`, `second`, and `third` respectively (where `first` is the company he prefers most).

You also know that Utkarsh has received offers from exactly two of these three companies, given you as characters xxx and yyy.

Utkarsh will always accept the offer from whichever company is highest on his preference list. Which company will he join?


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains three different space-separated characters: `first`, `second`, and `third`, denoting Utkarsh's order of preference among the three companies.
The second line of each test case contains two space-separated characters xxx and yyy, the companies from which Utkarsh has received offers.


Output Format
For each test case, print one line containing a single character, the company whose offer Utkarsh will accept.


The output is not case sensitive, so if the answer is AAA, both aaa and AAA will be accepted.
constraints: first, second, third are a permutation of {A,B,C}
x and y are two different characters from {A,B,C}
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains three distinct characters separated by spaces: first second third – Utkarsh's preference order (most to least preferred). The second line contains two distinct characters separated by spaces: x y – the companies that have made offers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single character on its own line – the company whose offer Utkarsh will accept (the one that appears earlier in his preference list).

sampleTestCases.input:
2
A B C
A B
B C A
A C
sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Preference order A B C, offers A and B. A is higher in the list, so answer A.\nTest case 2: Preference order B C A, offers A and C. C appears before A, so answer C.

solution.approach: For each test case we read the three preference characters in order. Then we read the two offered companies. The answer is the first character among the preference list that matches either of the offered companies. This can be done with a few equality checks in O(1) time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored.
